content:
Types of Cache
Caching exists at multiple layers. The closer the cache is to the user, the more latency it removes.
Browser
↓
CDN
↓
Reverse Proxy
↓
Application Cache
↓
Database Cache
↓
Database1. Browser Cache
Stores static resources on the user’s device.
Examples:

• CSS

• JavaScript

• Images

• Fonts
Benefits:

• No network request

• Fastest possible response

• Saves bandwidth
Common headers:

• Cache-Control

• Expires

• ETag
2. CDN Cache
Stores content at geographically distributed edge servers.
User (India)
↓
Nearest CDN Edge
↓
Origin ServerBest suited for:

• Images

• Videos

• Static assets

• Downloads
Benefits:

• Reduced latency

• Lower origin traffic

• Better global performance
3. Reverse Proxy Cache
Caches responses before they reach the application.
Client
↓
Nginx / Varnish
↓
Application
↓
DatabaseSuitable for:

• Public APIs

• Static pages

• Anonymous traffic
Benefits:

• Application receives fewer requests.

• Response time improves significantly.
4. Application Cache
Managed directly by the application.
Common technologies:

• Redis

• Memcached
Used for:

• User profiles

• Product details

• Session data

• API responses

• Configuration
This is the most commonly discussed cache in system design interviews.
5. Database Cache
Many databases cache frequently accessed pages internally.
Examples:

• Buffer Pool

• Query Cache (database dependent)

• Page Cache
Application developers typically have limited control over this layer.

Cache Eviction
When cache memory becomes full, some items must be removed.
This process is called eviction.
A good eviction policy keeps the most valuable data in memory.
1. Least Recently Used (LRU)
Removes the item that has not been accessed for the longest time.
Most Recent
A B C DLeast Recent
↓
Remove D
Advantages:

• Simple

• Effective for temporal locality

• Most widely used
Best for:

• General-purpose caching
2. Least Frequently Used (LFU)
Removes the item with the fewest accesses.
A → 100 accesses
B → 80 accesses
C → 2 accesses
Remove CAdvantages:

• Keeps popular items longer
Best for:

• Stable access patterns
3. First In First Out (FIFO)
Removes the oldest inserted item.
Inserted:
A
B
C
↓
Remove AAdvantages:

• Very simple
Disadvantages:

• Ignores usage frequency
4. Random Eviction
Selects an item randomly.
Advantages:

• Extremely cheap

• No metadata maintenance
Used when eviction speed matters more than accuracy.
5. TinyLFU (Overview)
TinyLFU combines recent access patterns with historical frequency using a compact frequency estimator.
Benefits:

• Higher hit ratio than traditional LRU in many real-world workloads

• Better memory efficiency

• Widely adopted in modern caching libraries
Used by libraries such as Caffeine for JVM applications.
For interviews, know that TinyLFU aims to make smarter admission and eviction decisions than plain LRU or LFU.
